metadata.seed: That I'm not the one in the wrong. You know, I. I'm extremely good at my job. I mean, I am the best employee that they have ever had. Not just currently, just. I mean, just ever. I make no errors. So how can I be wrong when it comes to these circumstances with my coworkers?

Can you think of a situation where you might have missed something or received feedback that suggested a different perspective? What evidence supports the idea that you never make errors, and what evidence might suggest otherwise?

patient.query: I wouldn't say I feel confident all the time... I mean, sure, I care deeply about my work, and I want to do well. But there have been moments where I’ve realized I misjudged something or missed an important detail. It’s frustrating, and I tend to dwell on those mistakes, you know? I often feel like I should have seen it coming, and I worry that it means I’m not good enough at what I do. It’s like there’s this constant pressure

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure after each slip. When you think about those moments, what evidence do you have that you’re not good enough, and can you recall times when you handled similar situations well? Noticing a mistake doesn’t automatically mean you’re failing—many people find ways to learn and grow from them.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do put a lot of pressure on myself to always get things right. But when I think back, it’s hard to remember the good moments. I focus so much on what went wrong. I mean, there were times I handled things well, but they feel overshadowed by my mistakes. It’s like the mistakes linger in my mind more. Sometimes, I feel like I'm just waiting for that next slip-up, like it's inevitable.

I really want to believe that when I mess up, it’s a chance to learn, but it feels like I’m just waiting for the next thing to go wrong. It makes it tough to feel proud of what I do.

If you could talk to a close friend about this, what might they say about the successes you’ve already achieved?

patient.query: I guess if I had to think about evidence, I’d probably point to some feedback I’ve gotten that didn’t go the way I hoped. It’s like I hold onto those comments, and they replay in my head. But if I think about what a friend might say, I know they’d probably remind me of the projects I nailed or the times I helped my team.
